candidate_problems.text: How can 'effective semantic control' be defined and measured non-circularly across organisms? The conjecture hinges on a variable that is more explanatory than genome size, but it remains unclear how to operationalize it so that it predicts developmental and organismal complexity rather than merely redescribing it after the fact. A worthwhile problem is to formulate metrics that separate load-bearing regulatory control from inert sequence, while accounting for dependence on cellular and environmental interpretive systems.

What are the causal boundaries between genomic information and the preexisting interpretive machinery that makes it effective? The conjecture says genomes act only within transcriptional, cellular, physical, and ecological contexts, but this raises an unresolved partitioning problem: which aspects of developmental control are genuinely specified by the genome versus inherited in the cell, organism, or environment? Exploring this could clarify whether the genome is a compact program, an index into a larger system, or only one component in a distributed inheritance architecture.

Under what conditions can compact regulatory architectures generate open-ended organismal complexity, and what are the limits of that compression? The conjecture analogizes genomes to short programs producing rich outputs, but it does not specify when iterative developmental rules actually scale into robust complexity rather than fragility, degeneracy, or repetitive form. A strong new problem is to identify the structural features of developmental networks—modularity, recursion, feedback, reuse, error correction—that make compressed genomic control sufficient for large phenotypic and behavioral differences.
